retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Pterygomandibular Space
retrieved_docs.content: The pterygomandibular space is mainly made up of loose areolar connective tissue. [9] The aim of an IAN block (IANB) is to deliver the anesthetic solution to the vicinity of the IAN in the pterygomandibular space. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Medial Pterygoid Muscle -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Utmost care is necessary during the surgical removal of the third molar in the mandibular arch as the tooth can easily get displaced into the space present between the ramus of the mandible and medial pterygoid muscle. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Pterygomandibular Space
retrieved_docs.content: The lingula is a bony projection anterior to the mandibular foramen. It is important to deliver the IANB above the lingula because the sphenomandibular ligament attaches to the lingula, and this fibrous ligament can prevent the effective diffusion of the anesthetic solution. [9] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Medial Pterygoid Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Infection in the mandibular teeth more frequently involves the lower compartment of the masticatory space, which contains medial pterygoid muscle; this could result in pterygoid muscle abscess. Since both masticator space and infratemporal fossa share the medial pterygoid muscle, there are chances of the potential spread of infection from this space to the infratemporal fossa.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Pterygomandibular Space
retrieved_docs.content: The pterygomandibular space contains the inferior alveolar neurovascular bundle, the lingual nerve, and the sphenomandibular ligament. [9] It is bound by the lateral pterygoid muscle superiorly, the medial pterygoid muscle inferiorly and medially, the mandibular ramus laterally, the parotid gland posteriorly, and the pterygomandibular raphe anteriorly. The pterygomandibular raphe is formed by the junction of the buccinator and superior constrictor muscles. [9] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Abscess -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The anatomy of the neck is quite complex. The 2 main fascial planes are (1) superficial cervical fascia and (2) deep cervical fascia. The deep neck spaces include the following: Parapharyngeal space (ie, lateral pharyngeal space/pharyngomaxillary space/pterygomaxillary space/pterygoid-pharyngeal space) Retropharyngeal space Prevertebral space Danger space Masticator space Submandibular space Carotid space, pretracheal space Peritonsillar space Parotid space Temporal space

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Medial Pterygoid Muscle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The lingual nerve runs in an anteroinferior direction lateral to the belly of the medial pterygoid muscle and medial to the mandible. The area between the medial pterygoid muscle and superior constrictor muscle is referred to as the prestyloid space, which is filled with fat.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Venous drainage occurs via the pterygoid venous plexus in the retrodiscal region, which communicates with the internal maxillary, sphenopalatine, middle meningeal, deep temporal, masseteric, and inferior alveolar veins. Lymphatic drainage of the TMJ is variable, particularly in pathological conditions when lymph nodes may increase in number. Under normal circumstances, the lymphatic vessels associated with the TMJ primarily drain toward the submandibular triangle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Maxillary Arteries -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural hematomas form from trauma to the middle meningeal artery at the pterion. The artery is within the periosteal dura and thus leaking of the vessel is under high pressure and causes a hematoma formation between the layers of the dura. More often epidural hematomas occur in younger patients aged 20 to 30 years old and less often in 50 to 60-year-old patients because older patients have dura that more tightly adheres to the cranium and it is less likely for a hematoma to form between the layers of the dura.  The patient initially experiences a brief loss of consciousness following a characteristic and almost pathognomonic “lucid interval,” where the patient can function. However, as the expanding hematoma continues to increase intracranial pressure, the patient then loses consciousness again with a high risk of mortality and morbidity. The most common modality used for evaluation of epidural hematoma is a non-contrast CT scan because it is more likely to show an acute bleed. The characteristic lens-shaped lesion, which does not cross suture lines, is how an epidural often presents. Epidural hematomas are considered neurosurgical emergencies and require immediate evacuation and relief of pressure off the brain. The gold standard is craniotomy; however, burr hole placement is also acceptable if the patient is not in a setting equipped to do a craniotomy. [11]

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Medial Pterygoid Muscle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The medial pterygoid muscle receives vascular supply from the maxillary artery through its pterygoid branches and by the facial artery through its muscular branches. The pterygoid branches of the maxillary artery are 2 or 3 in number. They run in an anterior and inferior direction to enter the medial aspect of the medial pterygoid muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Pterygopalatine Fossa -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The maxilla, palatine, and sphenoid bones bound the pterygopalatine fossa. The maxillary sinus' posterior wall comprises the fossa's anterior border, while the palatine bone constitutes its floor and anteromedial wall. The sphenoid bone, specifically the sphenoid body and palatine orbital process, comprise the superior boundary. The maxillary antrum's posterior wall forms the anterior boundary. The pterygoid process and the sphenoid's greater wing constitute the posterior boundary. The medial boundary is composed of the palatine bone and nasal mucoperiosteum. The temporalis muscle comprises the fossa's lateral boundary via the pterygomaxillary fissure.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Pterygomandibular Space
retrieved_docs.content: A crest of thick bone, known as the crista endocoronoidea, forms the anterior border of the sulcus colli, and it should be contacted during IANB administration. Contacting this bony protrusion helps ensure that the anesthetic solution is delivered adjacent to the IAN, without mechanical injury to the nerve and superior to the lingula. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Piriform Fossae
retrieved_docs.content: Any pooling of saliva, fullness, or masses seen here requires further investigation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumatization of the articular tubercle represents an anatomical variant of the TMJ. Bone cavities or air cells are present in the root of the zygomatic arch and the tubercle or eminence of the temporal bone. These cavities may occur unilaterally or bilaterally. Normal development includes the reabsorption of the cavities during puberty. However, reabsorption may not occur in some cases. This anatomical variation does not appear to affect TMJ function or symptomatology negatively. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is identified as a disorder of the nasal septum (اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي), representing bleeding that originates from the front portion of the nasal cavity and is commonly associated with structural or mucosal abnormalities of the septum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2878
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx are classified as a subcategory of عمليات الأنف, indicating that these examinations are considered part of the broader group of nasal operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924
retrieved_docs.content: not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region. It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: or discectomy. In addition, excision is employed in therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is utilized in plastic surgery of the nose, illustrating its wide applicability across multiple organ systems within the hierarchical clinical‑action framework guiding diagnostic and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1857
retrieved_docs.content: Hypromelóza, a hydroxypropyl methylcellulose compound, is classified as a member of the viscoelastic substances used as ophthalmological surgical aids, indicating its role in maintaining ocular space and protecting intra‑ocular structures during eye surgery. In addition to this specific ophthalmic application, hypromelóza is also described as having a broader relationship to nasal vasoconstrictors, suggesting that it shares pharmacologic or formulation characteristics with agents that cause constriction of nasal blood vessels. This dual association highlights hypromelóza’s relevance both in eye‑surgery contexts and in the wider category of compounds related to nasal vasoconstriction, underscoring the importance of understanding its multifunctional properties for comprehensive patient care.
